intakes.household_composition: single parent with two school-age children

turns.text: Hi Marisol, thanks for coming in this morning. Before we get into anything, can I get you some water or coffee?
Water's fine. I'm sorry, I haven't really slept. I got the papers Friday.
No need to apologize. Take your time. Can you tell me what the papers said?
It's a forcible detainer. Court date is next Tuesday. I'm three months behind. My hours got cut at the warehouse in February and I just couldn't catch up.
Okay, that's a tight timeline but it's workable. Who's in the household with you?
My two kids. My son's in third grade, my daughter just turned six. Their dad isn't in the picture.
Got it. And the apartment — do you want to try to stay there, or are you thinking it's time to move regardless?
I want to stay. The kids are settled. I just need someone to help me talk to the landlord. He won't return my calls anymore.
That makes sense. We have a tenant attorney who comes in on Wednesdays — I want to get you on her calendar before your court date. Have you ever applied for emergency rental assistance?
I tried in January. Got denied because I didn't have one of the forms. I never figured out which one.
We can re-do that application together today. The denial doesn't bar you from reapplying. How much is the back rent?
It's around twenty-two hundred. Plus late fees, but the lease says those cap out.
Okay. That's within range for the program. Do the kids know what's going on?
My son knows something's wrong. I haven't told him details. I just want to keep them in their school.
We'll do everything we can. Let's start the paperwork and I'll call the attorney's office while you fill it out.
Mr. Fakeman? I'm Reina, one of the caseworkers here. The hospital social worker called and said you'd be coming over.
Yeah. They discharged me about an hour ago. I walked.
That's a hike. How are you feeling right now?
Tired. My legs are swollen up again. They gave me the water pills but I can't take them if I don't have a bathroom.
That's a real problem and I hear you. Where have you been staying the last few weeks?
The encampment by the rail yard, mostly. Sometimes the bus station when it rains. I'm not gonna lie, I've been to the ER six, seven times this year.
You don't have to justify that to me. Bodies break down when there's no safe place to rest. Are you on any medications besides the diuretic?
Heart stuff. Blood thinner. There's an inhaler too but I lost it.
Okay. Tonight my priority is getting you a bed where you can elevate your legs and take your meds on schedule. We have a medical respite partnership — six beds, and I think one's open.
I've heard about that place. They make you do groups?
There's a daily check-in with the nurse, but no group therapy requirement. It's medically focused.
Alright. I'll try it. I just don't want to end up back in the ER tomorrow.
That's exactly what we're trying to prevent. Let me make the call. While I do, can you write down or tell me the medications you remember?
I've got the discharge paper in my bag. It's all on there.
Janelle, you're safe here. The doors are locked from the outside. Take whatever time you need.
He doesn't know I left. I told him I was going to my sister's. My sister doesn't even live here anymore.
Okay. Is the little one with you?
She's right here. She's two. She slept through most of the drive.
She's beautiful. Are either of you hurt right now, anything that needs medical attention?
I'm okay. He grabbed my arm but it's just bruised. He didn't hit her. He's never hit her.
I'm glad she wasn't hurt, and I'm sorry about your arm. Have you been to a DV shelter before, or is this your first time reaching out?
First time. I called the hotline last year but I hung up. I wasn't ready.
Reaching out is hard, and you did it tonight. That matters. Our DV partner has a confidential location — I can have someone meet us in about twenty minutes for transport. Is that okay?
Yeah. Yes. Is there a way he can find out where it is?
The address isn't public. Your file here will be flagged confidential — even within our coalition, only the DV advocate sees it. Your name won't show in the regular system.
Okay. Thank you. I have her diaper bag and my phone. That's it.
That's enough for tonight. They'll have clothes, formula, anything she needs. Let me start the warm handoff.

intakes.presenting_issue: youth aging out of foster care

turns.text: Hey Trey, come on in. Your worker from the independent living program said you might stop by.
Yeah she said y'all do housing stuff. My placement ends on the thirtieth.
Right, your eighteenth birthday. How are you feeling about it?
I don't know. Kinda ready to be done with the system. But also like, where am I supposed to go?
That's the right question to be asking, and it's the one we can actually work on. Are you working or in school?
I work part-time at the car wash. I was gonna start at the community college in the fall but I haven't done the financial aid yet.

Okay. Good news — there's a transitional housing program specifically for former foster youth, up to age 21. Bedroom of your own, you pay 30% of income for rent, case management is required but light.
How long is the wait?
For aging-out youth there's a priority track. Usually three to ten days if you have your documents ready. Do you have your ID, social security card, birth certificate?
I have my ID and my social. The birth certificate I'm not sure. My worker might have it.
We can request a copy today, takes about 48 hours. While we wait on that, let's get the housing application started. Any pets, any partner who'd be moving in with you?
Nah. Just me.
Easy. And just so I know what you want — is staying in the area important, or are you open to other towns in the network?
I'd rather stay close. My little brother's still in care here and I want to be able to see him.
Got it. We'll prioritize local. Let's pull up the application.

intakes.household_composition: two working adults plus teenage daughter

turns.text: Hi Carla, thanks for making the appointment. I understand from your intake form you're not in a crisis right now but you wanted to talk through some options?
Right. We're not behind on anything. But the rent went up two hundred dollars on the renewal and I just don't see how we keep doing this.
That's a really common situation and I'm glad you came in before things got tight. Tell me about the household.
It's me, my husband, and our daughter. She's fifteen. He drives a route for a beverage company, I'm a CNA at the nursing home off the parkway.
Two solid jobs. What does the rent look like now after the increase?
Eighteen-fifty for a two bedroom. With utilities we're at like twenty-one hundred a month. We bring home about thirty-six hundred between us.
So you're at roughly 58% of take-home on housing. That's well past cost-burdened. Is your current building privately owned or part of a larger management company?
Big company. They own a bunch of complexes around here.
Got it. Have you ever applied for a housing choice voucher or looked at the LIHTC tax-credit properties?
The voucher list I heard is closed. Tax credit, no, I don't really know what that is.

Voucher list is closed for general applicants right now, but there are tax-credit properties where rent is capped based on income — no waitlist at a couple of them. With your earnings you'd likely qualify and the rent on a two-bedroom would be around eleven to twelve hundred.
That would change everything. What's the catch?

Honest answer — the units go fast when they open up, the application process is paperwork-heavy, and not all the buildings are in neighborhoods people want. But some are fine. Want me to pull up which ones have openings or short waits this month?
Yes, please. And can my daughter stay in her school if we move?
Depends on the district line, but the school system has a transfer process for families relocating mid-year. We can map the addresses against her school zone before you apply anywhere.
Marcus, welcome. I see your re-entry coordinator referred you. When's your release date?
Next Thursday. They let me come over today on a pass for housing planning.
Smart to start now. How long have you been in?
Almost four years this stretch. Before that I was out for about eighteen months.
Okay. And before incarceration, where were you living?
With my mom. She passed in 2024. House went to my sister and her husband and they've made it clear there's no room.
I'm sorry about your mom. That's a lot to come home to. What's the offense on your record, just so I know what programs you're eligible for?
It's a drug charge. Possession with intent. No violence on my record, no sex offense.
That actually opens up most of our options. The hardest doors are closed for violent and sex offenses; you're not in either category. Are you on parole or probation after release?
Parole. Two years. I have to report within 24 hours and I need an approved address.

Right, that's the piece we have to nail down before Thursday. We have a re-entry transitional housing program — six month stay, sober environment, employment support. They work directly with parole to approve the address.
Six months sober environment, like no drinking either?
Correct. Random testing. Is that going to work for you, honestly?
Yeah. I did the program inside. I'm not trying to mess this up.
Good. Let's start the application now and I'll fax the conditional acceptance letter to your re-entry coordinator by end of day so parole has it.
Mr. Paperton, thanks for coming in. I see you served — Army?
Yeah. Late seventies into the early eighties. Mostly Germany.
Thank you for your service. What brings you in today?
I've been staying with a buddy since November. His wife's getting tired of me. I tried the VA but they said something about my discharge code and I got confused and walked out.
That happens a lot, and it's fixable more often than people think. Do you remember the type of discharge?
It was a general, under honorable conditions. Not dishonorable. There was an incident at the end but it wasn't a court martial.

Okay — that's important. General under honorable usually qualifies you for HUD-VASH and SSVF. The intake person at the VA may have been looking at the wrong line. We have a veterans' navigator on staff Tuesdays and Thursdays who can go with you to the appointment.
Someone would actually go with me?
Yes. He's a veteran himself. He knows the building, knows the staff, and he won't let you get bounced.
That'd be a help. I'm not good at those places anymore. The fluorescent lights, the lines.
Understood. While we work on the VA side, are you safe at your friend's place this week?
Yeah. She's annoyed but she's not gonna throw me out. I just don't want to wear out the welcome.
Got it. So we're not in same-night territory, we're working a 30-to-60 day plan to get you into HUD-VASH. Sound right?
Sounds right. What do I need to bring Tuesday?
Your DD-214 if you have it, your ID, and any VA correspondence even if it's the denial. If you don't have the DD-214, the navigator can request it — takes about ten days.
Reina, I'm Marcus, the overnight caseworker. The mobile crisis team called ahead. How are you doing right now?
I don't know. I'm tired. They said I had to come here or go to the hospital and I don't want the hospital.
Okay. You're not in the hospital, you're with me. Are you having thoughts of hurting yourself right now, in this moment?
Not right now. Earlier today, yes. That's why I called. But not right now.
I'm really glad you called. That took a lot. Are you connected with a therapist or psychiatrist?
I was. I lost my insurance in February when I lost the job. I have meds for like four more days.

Okay. So we have a few things to stabilize — somewhere to sleep tonight, getting your meds bridged, and reconnecting you to a prescriber. The shelter has a quiet wing tonight — only three other people in it. Would that feel manageable?
Three is okay. The big rooms — I can't do the big rooms.
Then we'll do the quiet wing. Tomorrow morning the community mental health team has a same-day intake at 9 a.m. They have a sliding scale and they'll bridge your prescription that day.
Same day? Last time I tried it was a six week wait.
They opened a same-day slot in February specifically for crisis referrals like yours. I'll walk you over at 8:45.
Okay. Thank you. I'm sorry I'm a mess.
You're not a mess. You're a person having a hard night who made the right call. Let's get you settled.

intakes.household_composition: father seeking reunification with two children

turns.text: Mr. Synthwell, thanks for waiting. I know the lobby was full. What's going on?

My kids are at my mother-in-law's. CPS placed them there in January when their mom went into treatment. I have a hearing next week and the judge said I need a stable address with bedrooms for the kids before they'll consider placing them with me.
Okay. So this isn't about whether you have somewhere to sleep tonight — you do — it's about getting an apartment that meets the court's standard. Is that right?
Right. I'm at a friend's. I have a bed. But there's no room for the kids and the friend has a record so the caseworker said no anyway.
Got it. How old are the kids and same gender or different?
Boy who's nine, girl who's six. So they need separate rooms once she's a little older but right now the worker said one bedroom for them is fine if I'm in another.
Two-bedroom minimum then. What's your income look like?
I do HVAC. About fifty-two thousand a year. My credit is rough though, and I have an eviction from 2022.

The eviction is the biggest barrier on the private market. We have a rapid rehousing program with a landlord risk-mitigation fund — they'll cover up to two months rent if a landlord takes a chance, and we have four landlords who specifically work with reunification cases.
Four landlords. Real ones, not slumlords?
Real ones. We inspect the units before we refer. They're not luxury but they're decent and they pass CPS walkthroughs.
How fast can this happen? My hearing is the fourteenth.

If we apply today, I can probably have you viewing units by Wednesday or Thursday and signing a lease early next week. I can also write a letter for the hearing documenting that placement is in progress, in case you sign after the fourteenth.
That letter would matter. The judge said she needed something concrete.
Then let's start there. I'll need pay stubs, ID, the case number, and the name of the CPS worker.
Brittany, come sit down. I'm Tasha. Take your time.
I'm sick. I'm trying. I went to detox on Friday but I left the second day. I came here because I don't have anywhere and I'm pregnant and I can't keep doing this.
Okay. First thing — I hear you, and I'm not going anywhere. How far along are you?
About twenty weeks. I've had two prenatal appointments. They know I use.
Good that you're connected with prenatal care. That's a real strength. What were you using, if you're comfortable telling me?
Fentanyl mostly. Sometimes meth. I haven't used since yesterday morning and I'm starting to feel it.

Okay. Withdrawal during pregnancy needs medical management — going cold turkey isn't actually safe for the baby. There's a program at the medical center, MAT for pregnant women, inpatient. They have a partnership with us and they take same-day admits.
Inpatient? Like locked?

No, voluntary. You can leave but they'd really prefer you don't, obviously. It's a women-only floor. Average stay is about three weeks, and they discharge you to a recovery house that takes pregnant women and moms with infants.
A recovery house that takes the baby after?
Yes. Up to two years, mom and baby together. Most of the women who complete the inpatient piece transition there.
Nobody told me about that before.
It's a newer program. Can I call them right now while you're sitting with me?
Yes. Please. Before I change my mind.
I'm dialing. Stay right here. If you start feeling worse before they get here, tell me — we have a nurse on call.
